evidence: goseg/rectify/rectify.go defines four runtime interfaces plus four adapter structs whose methods are direct pass-through calls (for example, lines 50-52, 58-60, 69-83, 94-116), then RectifyRuntime adds another pass-through layer (lines 186-244).

goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go and goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go both build large function-pointer runtime bags and then manually re-merge/validate selected callbacks, increasing indirection without introducing domain policy.

goseg/config/config_update_registry.go uses reflection and string field names (`FieldByName`, generic get/set by string) to mutate `ConfPatch`, which hides real config boundaries and weakens interface honesty.

Transition handlers build large imperative workflows inline (for example `runStartramToggleWithRuntime` and `runStartramEndpointWithRuntime`) combining state reads, mutation, container lifecycle, and event emission in one function body.

Config patching relies on string field names + reflection (`patchFieldRefByName`, `getConfigPatchValueByName`, `setConfigPatchValueByName`) that must stay synchronized with struct field identifiers across embedded patch structs.

Runtime seam definitions are split into many callback-bag structs with overlapping responsibility (`RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeStartupOps`, `RuntimeConfigOps`, `runtimeSeamRegistry`), increasing coupling and making behavior assembly hard to reason about.

Rectify mixes wrapped and raw propagation in the same flow: `applyTransitionCompletion` wraps snapshot failures (`loading startram settings`) but returns raw `UpdateConfig` errors (`goseg/rectify/rectify_services.go:53-55`, `62-64`, `71-73`), and `Reconcile` returns raw snapshot errors while joining both contextualized and non-contextualized per-ship errors (`95-100`, `109`, `154`, `161`).

Docker subsystem uses incompatible failure contracts: `dockerListenerWithContext`/`dockerSubscriptionHandler` return errors to terminate `StartDockerSubsystem`, but `check502Loop` logs and continues on most failures without surfacing them (`goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go:38-59`, `115-151`; `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_health.go:52-55`, `61-64`, `68-70`, `82-84`, `99-101`).

Runtime dependency misconfiguration errors are not represented consistently: `RuntimeSnapshotOps` returns plain `errors.New(...)` strings while adjacent orchestration/importer paths use `seams.MissingRuntimeDependency(...)` sentinels (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:70-95`; `goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go:22-24`, `39-41`, `95-100`; `goseg/importer/importer.go:97-120`).

WiFi service nil handling is inconsistent and unsafe: several methods return explicit nil-service errors, but `ConstructWifiInfo` dereferences `service.state` before checking `service == nil`, creating a panic path instead of an error contract (`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go:39-43`, `46-53`, `94-97`, `124-127`).

findings.summary: Rectify runtime layers stack multiple pass-through abstractions that add call depth but little behavioral leverage.
evidence: In goseg/rectify/rectify.go, adapter methods and RectifyRuntime methods mostly forward directly to stored function pointers or interfaces (e.g., `rectifyUrbitRuntimeAdapter` methods and `RectifyRuntime.UpdateConfig/UrbitConf/Conf`).
Runtime construction in goseg/rectify/rectify.go wraps orchestration runtime callbacks into adapter structs, then resolves/merges them again before each public method call.
goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go repeats the same pattern via `workflowRuntime` + `resolveWorkflowRuntime` manual field-by-field merge for embedded runtime ops.
findings.suggestion: Collapse to one boundary abstraction per use-case: inject a narrower orchestration interface directly into rectify/workflow, remove adapter structs that only forward, and keep merge logic at constructor time rather than per method call.

findings.summary: Function-pointer runtime bags have become wide pseudo-interfaces, signaling under-modeled domain boundaries.
evidence: goseg/importer/importer.go splits runtime into `importerConfigRuntime`, `importerUploadRuntime`, and `importerProvisionRuntime`, but each contains many callback fields and large nil-validation ladders (lines ~170-261).

goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go similarly models startup as callback-heavy structs (`startupInitCoreSubsystemRuntime`, `startupBootstrapRuntimeFns`) with explicit missing-callback audits rather than cohesive service contracts.
goseg/main.go introduces another callback bag (`bootstrapRuntime`) and merge logic (`resolveBootstrapRuntime`) that repeats the same DI style instead of reusing a stable abstraction.
findings.suggestion: Replace callback bags with a small set of role-focused interfaces (for example: `StartupInitializer`, `ImportProvisioner`, `TransitionPublisher`) and pass concrete dependencies through constructors; keep per-test stubs at interface granularity instead of dozens of callback fields.

findings.summary: Config patch abstraction relies on reflection and string field names, creating a leaky, weakly-typed contract.
evidence: goseg/config/config_update_registry.go reads/writes patch fields via `reflect.ValueOf(...).Elem().FieldByName(field)` and string `patchField` entries, so correctness depends on string alignment rather than compile-time contracts.
The same registry then mutates embedded sub-configs via generic apply closures, while `structs.SysConfig` in goseg/structs/configs.go exposes many embedded domains through one aggregate type.
goseg/defaults/defaults.go builds the full `SysConfig` aggregate with all domains, reinforcing broad mutable surface area behind the patch abstraction.
findings.suggestion: Refactor patch application to typed domain commands (connectivity/startram/runtime/etc.) with compile-time field accessors; avoid reflection-based field routing for core config mutation paths.

findings.summary: Startram/urbit transition functions mix orchestration, policy checks, persistence, and side effects in single handlers.
evidence: `runStartramToggleWithRuntime` both decides policy (`settings.WgOn` branches), mutates system config (`UpdateConfTypedFn`), controls containers, dispatches urbit payloads, and rebuilds minio-related containers in one closure.
`buildToggleMinIOLinkSteps` performs runtime discovery, external service account creation, click integration, and config persistence as one coupled step chain.
Several handlers repeat the same orchestration pattern (assemble steps, perform side effects, run template), but each re-implements domain logic inline instead of using reusable operation units.
findings.suggestion: Extract transition-specific command objects or dedicated use-case services (for example `WireguardToggleUseCase`, `MinIOLinkUseCase`) that each encapsulate one domain concern, then compose them in thin transition handlers that only map events to reusable operations.

findings.summary: Config patch mapping is stringly-typed across schema and registry, creating fragile cross-file synchronization points.
evidence: Patch fields are connected by raw string identifiers (`patchField: "EndpointURL"`, etc.) and resolved via `reflect.FieldByName` in `getConfigPatchValueByName`/`setConfigPatchValueByName`.
`ConfPatch` is composed through embedded patch structs, so field visibility/lookup correctness depends on exact exported field names and embedding behavior rather than compiler-checked selectors.

Domain apply functions are strongly typed (`UpdateConnectivityConfig`, etc.) but the parse/set path uses reflection, so renames or schema changes can silently stop applying updates without compile-time failure.
findings.suggestion: Replace string-based field lookups with typed accessors for every patch field (using `confPatchPointerRef`/explicit getter-setter functions), and generate or centralize key-to-accessor declarations so compiler checks enforce schema alignment.

findings.summary: Runtime operation surfaces are fragmented into overlapping callback bags, increasing indirection and reducing boundary clarity.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` defines many near-overlapping ops structs (`RuntimeStartupOps`, `RuntimeConfigOps`, `RuntimeLoadOps`, `RuntimeServiceOps`, etc.) that repeat related capabilities (config update, load operations, service deletes) across boundaries.
`defaultRuntimeStartupOps` reassembles functions by pulling from multiple other runtime builders, showing that ownership boundaries are not cohesive and require manual stitching.
Ship workflow handlers depend on broad runtime bundles where only subsets are needed, signaling interfaces are organized around implementation assembly rather than focused behavior contracts.
findings.suggestion: Converge runtime ops into a smaller set of cohesive interfaces aligned to use-cases (for example `StartramRuntime`, `ShipConfigRuntime`, `ContainerRuntime`) and inject only minimal interfaces per handler to reduce assembly duplication and boundary leakage.

findings.summary: Rectify transition/reconcile paths alternate between contextualized and raw errors.
evidence: `applyTransitionCompletion` wraps snapshot failures (`loading startram settings`) but returns raw `UpdateConfig` errors (`rectify_services.go:53-55`, `62-64`, `71-73`).
`Reconcile` returns raw errors from `StartramSettingsSnapshot`/`GetStartramConfig` (`95-100`) and joins both contextualized (`109`) and uncontextualized (`154`, `161`) errors in the same aggregate.
This creates inconsistent caller experience: some failures carry operation/patp context, others do not.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a reconcile/transition error-wrapping helper that always annotates operation and ship context (when applicable), and use it for every returned/joined error path in rectify runtime calls.

findings.summary: Docker health monitoring swallows operational errors while sibling loops fail fast.
evidence: `StartDockerSubsystem` returns on errors from listener/subscription handlers (`subsystem_events.go:38-59`, `115-151`).

`check502Loop` logs and `continue`s for repeated runtime failures (`GetShipStatus`, config load, network lookup, polling, recovery) without surfacing them (`subsystem_health.go:52-55`, `61-64`, `68-70`, `82-84`, `99-101`).
The same subsystem therefore treats comparable operational failures as fatal in one path and non-fatal/silent in another, making failure observability unpredictable.
findings.suggestion: Define one health-loop error policy (for example: return fatal/repeated failures via channel to `StartDockerSubsystem`, keep transient ones as warnings), and apply it consistently across listener/subscription/check502 paths.

findings.summary: Missing runtime dependency errors use incompatible types across orchestration paths.
evidence: `RuntimeSnapshotOps` returns ad-hoc `errors.New(...)` strings for missing callbacks (`runtime_ops.go:70-95`).
Neighboring modules use `seams.MissingRuntimeDependency(...)` for the same class of failure (`wireguard.go:22-24`, `39-41`, `95-100`; `importer.go:97-120`).
Callers cannot reliably classify misconfiguration errors with `errors.Is/As` because representation varies by module.
findings.suggestion: Standardize runtime-misconfiguration failures on a single typed/sentinel error contract (prefer `seams.MissingRuntimeDependency`) and update `RuntimeSnapshotOps` plus its call sites/tests accordingly.

findings.summary: WiFi service mixes nil-error contracts with a nil-deref panic path.
evidence: `IsWifiEnabled`, `ToggleDevice`, and `ApplyCaptiveRules` explicitly return nil-service errors (`wifi_runtime_api.go:39-43`, `94-97`, `124-127`).
`ConstructWifiInfo` dereferences `service.state` before nil-checking service (`wifi_runtime_api.go:46-53`), so a nil receiver panics instead of following the established error contract.
Tests cover many wifi error paths but do not cover nil `ConstructWifiInfo`, allowing this contract break to persist (`wifi_test.go`).
findings.suggestion: Move the nil check to the top of `ConstructWifiInfo` before any field access, and align the method contract with the rest of the service (either return an error or clearly enforce non-nil receiver semantics with tests).
